steps.title: Focus on Mercer's objection
steps.content: Karim concludes that life satisfaction dropped mainly when participants spent all their free time unproductively, such as by watching TV or playing games. Mercer would focus on the weakness in that label. He says there is no objective definition of productivity and that the same activity, such as reading, might count as productive for some people and idle for others.

steps.title: Eliminate mismatched responses
steps.content: Choice A accepts Karim's productivity framework instead of questioning it. Choice B says productivity has not been proved to contribute to life satisfaction, but Mercer's point is about how productivity is defined. Choice D would make Mercer label TV and games productive, when his claim is that such labels depend on individual judgment rather than a universal rule.

steps.title: Select the broad-assumption warning
steps.content: Choice C says Mercer would warn against an overly broad assumption because there is no clear consensus on distinguishing productive from unproductive activities. That directly applies Mercer's view to Karim's conclusion: calling certain forms of free time unproductive assumes a shared definition Mercer explicitly rejects. The answer is C.
